author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

In this book Augustin refutes the second letter which Petilianus wrote to him after having seen the first of Augustin’s earlier books. This letter had been full of violent language; and Augustin rather shows that the arguments of Petilianus had been deficient and irrelevant, than brings forward arguments in support of his own statements.

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

title: Chapter 42

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For what I just now said is put with the greatest clearness in that very epistle of mine, in answering which he has said nothing; and I would beg of you to listen for a few moments to what he there has done.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And although you are partisans of his, and hate us, yet, if you can, bear it with equanimity.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For in his former epistle, to the first portion of which—the only portion which had then come into our hands—I had in the first instance made my reply, he had so rested the hope that is found in baptism in the baptizer, as to say, "For everything consists of an origin and root; and if anything has not a head, it is nothing."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Since then Petilianus had said this, not wishing anything to be understood by the origin and root and head of baptizing a man, except the man by whom he might be baptized, I made a comment, and said "We ask, therefore, in a case where the faithlessness of the baptizer is undetected, if then the man whom he baptizes receives faith and not guilt? if then the baptizer is not his origin and root and head, who is it from whom he receives faith? where is the origin from which he springs? where is the root of which he is a shoot? where the head which is his starting-point?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Can it be that, when he who is baptized is unaware of the faithlessness of his baptizer, it is then Christ who is the origin and root and head?"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: This therefore I say and exclaim now also, as I did there as well: "Alas for human rashness and conceit!

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Why do you not allow that it is always Christ who gives faith, for the purpose of making a man a Christian by giving it?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Why do you not allow that Christ is always the origin of the Christian, that the Christian always plants his root in Christ, that Christ is the Head of the Christian?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Will it then be urged that, even where spiritual grace is dispensed to those that believe by the hands of a holy and faithful minister, it is still not the minister himself who justifies, but that One of whom it is said, ‘He justifieth the ungodly’?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But unless we admit this, either the Apostle Paul was the head and origin of those whom he had planted, or Apollos the root of those whom he had watered, rather than He who had given them faith in briefing; whereas the same Paul says, ‘I have planted, Apollos watered; but God gave the increase.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: So that neither is he that planteth anything, neither he that watereth; but God that giveth the increase.’

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Nor was the apostle himself their root, but rather He who says, ‘I am the vine, ye are the branches.’

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: How, too, could he be their head, when he says that ‘we, being many, are one body in Christ,’ and expressly declares in many passages that Christ Himself is the Head of the whole body?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Wherefore, whether a man receives the sacrament of baptism from a faithful or a faithless minister his whole hope is in Christ, that he fall not under the condemnation, that ‘Cursed is he that placeth his hope in man!’"
